speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October 25th, 1995. I am interviewing Mikhail Becker whose name at birth was Avrom Bekker. My name, interviewer, is Sandy Hoffman. The interview is taking place in Milwaukee, Wisconsin in the USA in English. Today is October 25th, 1995. And the survivor we are interviewing is Mikhail Becker. My name is Sandy Hoffman, and the interview is taking place in Milwaukee, Wisconsin in the United States of America. Would you please say your name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. My name is Mikhail-- first name M-I-K-H-A-I-L. And my last name is Becker-- B-E-C-K-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth, and would you also spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name at birth was Avrom, A-V-R-O-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the last name. How was that spelled?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same last name.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was spelled--?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-E-C-K-E-R. But in Russia it was spelled K-K instead. But it's just in the Russian language. But it always should be in English B-E-C-K-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. At the war time, especially when I was under the German occupation. It was during almost 18 month. And my first name was Ivan, I-V-A-N, and the last name was Demov, D-E-M-O-V.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My date of birth is August 4th, 1916.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am 79.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me the city and country you are from? And please spell the name of the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Actually, it's not a big city. It's a shtetl, we used to call it in yiddish. But let's say city. The city-- the name of the city is Pilyava-- P-I-L-Y-A-V-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the country was the-- was Russia. Then was it Russia. But the part of the country is Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know you have a little map here. And I'm going to let you tell us about this now, because I'd like to know about your life before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This is approximately.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you turn it like in front of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the city like I remember it, approximately from 1924 till '28. You may see. The population was about-- that's in my account-- 400-450 Jewish population, only Jewish. All around the city there are villages, [GESTURING TO MAP] Ukrainian villages, where there-- where there lived the Ukrainian population. OK. You can see this city [POINTING AT MAP] had three synagogues. It's a small city, but it had three synagogues. This is the one synagogue. It's for the-- maybe for the more rich and more educated people. [POINTING AT MAP] This is a shul, they called it, for the average people. And this is a [? batmadush. ?] It's like-- like a school. It was also like a-- let's say it's not a [? yishiba. ?] But it's like a [? yishiba. ?] But there's also the high holidays they-- they got the service in here. So at the high holidays, all-- all the-- all this-- the shuls, all the synagogues, they are occupied. All of them [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And I notice you have your home.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. My home is here. [POINTING AT MAP] Shall I tell you? You want me to tell you about my family?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Why don't I take that now? Thank you. Yes. Please tell me about your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my-- my home, it's the only one home which is not-- was not in city, but out of the city. It was on a farm. Because my father, he worked for many years, for many decades. He worked for a rich farmer, a landlord, as a manager. The-- the landlord himself, he lived in Warsaw, Poland. And he killed his free time in Paris and [CHUCKLING] in Europe. But once a year, he used to come up here and for some-- for some information. And my father used to give him information, what is going on in the farm. It was many acres-- maybe a couple of thousand of acres. I don't know exactly. And he should stay, in front of him, to stay. OK, for three days you couldn't sit, only to stay and to tell him what is going on on the farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So but after the revolution, 1917, so the-- the farm, the-- the landlord, he disappeared. Maybe he-- he was there, still in Poland. And we got a piece of land over there. So we settled there. We built a home there. And we lived there until the World War-- the second, World War II. Tell me your father's name and your mother's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Hirsh, Hershell Hirsh-- H-I-R-S-H. My mother's name was Ita-- I-T-A. My-- I was the only one son from the second marriage from both my parents. So my father got a family, before his first wife died, and seven children. And my mother got a family, her husband died, and two children. And I am-- I am the only one between my father and mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can you tell me your-- you brothers' and sisters' names? First tell me your-- your brothers and sisters from your father's side.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. From my father's side the oldest sister was Sura. She moved to the United States in 1920. The second one was [? Yanchil, ?] or [? Yakov. ?] He moved to the United States, I believe, in 1913. Now, another sister, Murel, she also emigrated to the United States in 19-- I believe, --15. And the other sisters, Sonya, and Girl, and Sura, they stayed until 1920. Sonya was married and-- and got a-- and got a boy, a child. The child was-- the boy was born in 1920. And then pretty soon they immigrate to the United States. He is the-- he is the now the survivor of the family, [? Paul ?] [? Maur ?], he lives [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So, we used to live at this same home-- it was another home there. It was an old home. Two halves, you know, like side by side. An old home it was. So in one half used to live my father, my mother, and I. And in the second half used to live Sonya with her husband [? Mortel ?] and [? Pasha, ?] this mean [INAUDIBLE], and my youngest sister Gitl. She's younger than all of them, but she's older than I am. She was older than I am. So the family emigrated in 1920. Another brother, [? Bertrich ?] his name was Boris, actually. He emigrated to Romania. Actually, his goal was to reach America. But he never reached America and he was-- he used to live in Romania.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in 1940 when the Soviet Union seized Moldavia, Moldavia was a part of Romania. So he got a citizen of the Soviet Union. He became a citizen of the Soviet Union. And then after the war we met with him, because he, too, used to live in the Soviet Union. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK this is-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that yes, what I want to say-- still want to say. At this shtetl it was a Jewish religious life. Always, always was a Jewish religious life. Everyone knows he is-- he is Jewish. He got to go at Saturday shabbat to the synagogue, Friday evening to the synagogue, and even in weekdays some of them. And at the holidays everyone goes. At the high holidays, pesach, everyone goes to the synagogues.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell me the names of your brothers and sisters from your mother's side?

speaker: interviewee
text: From my mother's side, two brothers. The older brother was David and the younger was Josef. So they both lived-- we lived together-- until, I believe, in 1929 David was taken to the service, to the Red Army. And then he came back. But he settled first, he settled in the city of Kiev. But then Josef was taken to the army. And he, too, settled there. So he, David, eventually he returned home-- Married and returned home. And Josef, he used to live in Kiev. And David was killed by the Nazis in 1941. Him and his family, his wife and two children. And they were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that your town had a Jewish population of-- of between--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I estimate it at, about 500.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, was it mainly a Jewish shtetl? Were there non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, it was only?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only a Jewish shtetl. Like I said. And all around have been villages with Ukrainian population.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you go to only Jewish schools, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, yes. When I was four, I didn't mention, when I was four I went to cheder where the education was only in-- in Hebrew. And then until nine [INAUDIBLE] I stayed there until nine. And then after 9 o'clock-- sorry-- after nine years I went to a public school, Ukrainian public. Ukrainian. And then a Yiddish school, actually. I went to a Yiddish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I finished the, it was a-- a great school, I finished the great school, four years. And then I went to another city. It's 10 kilometer from Pilyava. That's called [? Siyava ?], to a middle school. So, three more years I was there at the middle school. And then I returned back. It was in 1932. It was very hungry this year. And it was very hungry there in Russia. Because, you know, of Stalin, of the communists, of the new orders. And it was very hungry there. So I stayed one-- stayed one year home. And then in 1933 I went to Kiev to my brother. And I started to study in a technical school. The technical school I finished after four years. I finished in 1937.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I became a technician. It's called a technician, a technical about-- a structured technical-- a structural technician. And so I was-- I worked there. And then I went. I moved to a city [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH]. It's called. It's not far from Kiev. I got there a good job. And I worked there until January 1940, where I was taken to the Red Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's go back a little bit. You came from a very religious family.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your-- your life. Your religious life.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very strict. My family was very strictly religious. My father was also a cantor. And, I would say, a good cantor because before the high holidays he got many offers, you know, from-- not from only from these synagogues, but from other cities. So they actually he accept the best offer. And then he went for the high holidays. But, besides, I go to the cheder, and my father goes every evening-- sometimes in the morning when he was free, to the synagogue. This is at the weekdays. And on Friday evening, always I went with my father to the synagogue. And so it was on Shabbat. And so it was on high holidays. We-- we kept kosher. Everything was kosher. And, let's say, until I was 17, when I was 16, when I was still home, I didn't understand what-- what does it mean eating not kosher. I remember at the market, there was a marketplace in Pilyava. So every other Sunday all the farmers around the city they-- they came in with their products. And they used to-- to sell, you know, sausages. It was so smelled so good. But I go around, but I never tried. Because, you know, that was, that was the life, and that was my-- that like I was educated.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the holiday, it was so nice. My wife often asks me, what did you do in a small city? If-- if I were there, I would die. I said, no. It was so nice. It was so nice there, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, we kept all holidays. And my mom used to-- to bake, to prepare, you know, nice food, very delicious food. And we invited relatives, and just friends. And so they invited us for the holidays-- and not only for the holidays. Even on Shabbat.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I also remember [CLEARING THROAT] every Shabbat we invited some poor man for-- for dinner. That was every Shabbat. So this-- that was my life, you know. It used to be my life. And even after this life, it passed more 50 years, I was not religious. I was not. But now it's still in my blood, you know. And that's why when I came to the United States, now I go to an Orthodox. Shul. Because I used to. When I hear [NON-ENGLISH] and when I hear on the high holidays the cantor. And I close my eyes, I can hear my daddy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what your father was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you may see the-- the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, I don't want to know what he looked like. What-- what kind of a man was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was, for that time, he was born in 1861. At that time he was a very educated man. He knows several languages, and even the grammars. He knows Yiddish. He knows very well Hebrew. And he even he speaks Hebrew. And he knows Polish, Russian, and Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, like I mentioned, when the landlord came up, he talked in only Polish. You couldn't talk another language with him. Otherwise he would fire him. And all the books were written in Polish. And what about your mother? What was your mother like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother she was just home-- a home wife. And that's it. But she was much younger than my father-- 24 years younger. And we, I think they got a good life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to her first husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: He died. Naturally died. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and so-- and so died my father's wife. And then they married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any particular memories you have of your life at home that you could share? Anything that stands out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not really, not outstanding. I don't-- not outstanding memories. It was a life like-- like any others-- some other Jewish homes. I've got friends. But, it's completely changed, you know, when the Germans came.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of things did you do for recreation? As-- as a young boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: As young boy? Well, I used to go fishing. Reading. I liked to read. And playing, playing with kids, with-- with the boys and girls. Playing together. And we often played at the synagogues, you know. At the [INAUDIBLE] synagogue, we used to play there on the holidays. So it was a good life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, since there were not gentiles in your city, did you experience anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes and no. Let me tell you, the gentiles, the Ukrainians, most of them, they used to live with-- with us-- with the Jews. We are in freedom very good. So they need us. We need them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came up to the city with some product-- products. And they are-- we are also stores, retail stores, in the city where they can buy everything from the stores. So they need us. We need them.

speaker: interviewee
text: But some of them, yes. Yes. Some of them were anti-Semitic. And all the-- maybe I'll tell it later on. But only at the beginning of the war then you can-- then you can see-- you started to see the anti-Semites there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you never-- your family or you did not experience anti-Semitism before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not. As a matter of fact, we used to live in the village. The farm was in the village. And we got many friends-- many Ukrainian friends. And we lived very good with them. They invited us even, you know, for holidays, despite my, we did not eat there. But still they invited for weddings. Like I said, we did not eat there. But we have been there.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a period of time that you had a-- one of your-- a niece lived with you, did you say? In 1932 you had a niece who lived with you in your home for about a year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 19? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes, oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she was a small, small child.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen? Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did that happen? It was a very difficult time then in-- in Russia and the Soviet Union. It was a hunger-- very hunger year, there was no food. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's from my-- the daughter of my brother Josef from my mom's side.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- they brought her to us. It was easier, you know, in the village, in the shtetl, to live. And she was for a year with us. Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, her name was Nelly-- N-E-L-Y, or maybe E-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did anyone else live with you at all? Did you have other-- your had mentioned you had an uncle, or an aunt that lived-- or rather it was your brother and his wife lived also in your home? Or near your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to us. And then they moved to [INAUDIBLE]. It's 10 kilometer there. So where he was killed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the war began, how did things, in 1939, how did things begin to change? What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war began, I was in the army. I was serving two years in the army, I was serving my duty there. The second year. So actually-- and that was near the city of Rowne. It was in a-- in a small-- in a small village there. The regiment where-- where I used to serve my duty was deployed in a small village near-- near city of Rowne at the Ukrainian. And so I didn't see the civil life, you know, of the population, because it passed a year and a half I was in the army already.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did-- when did you join the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: In January 1940. And the war begins with Russia on June 22nd, 1941. So it's almost a year and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what-- you got the news about the Germans going into Poland, and when they first marched in in '39.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what were the thoughts of your family at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, I wasn't with the family at that time. And, as a matter of fact, my mom died in 1937. So my father was all alone. And he was an old man. He was 79 then, or maybe 78. So I don't know what is his thoughts. Actually, it was fear. I know what it was everywhere. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] When the war begins, and it was Sunday. I remember that Sunday. And we didn't know. Even in the army, we didn't know the war begins. But the war begins at 4 o'clock in the morning. And we-- we were told only at 11 o'clock in the morning that there is a war between the Germans and the Russians. Before they have been friends. Before they have been allies. But it was false allies. It wasn't allies at all. It was enemies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you drafted into the army? How did you get into the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: How? Well, it's-- I have to go into the army. It's a must go. So I wouldn't go by myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what unit were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you have a name to the unit that you were in in the service?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, yes. I was-- I was serving my duty in a artillery regiment down near the city of Rowne, like I said. And on the third day of the war, our regiment and our division, they joined, you know, the defensive, the defense army. And-- and we are defeated at the same night. We have been defeated. So until morning, there was no regiment. There was no division. And everyone fled to the rear, you know, to the east.
